perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not, however, include any information which (i) was publicly known and made generally available in the public domain prior to the time of disclosure by the disclosing party; (ii) becomes publicly known and made generally available after disclosure by the disclosing party to the receiving party through no action or inaction of the receiving party; (iii) is already in the possession of the rec eiving party at the time of disclosure by the disclosing party as shown by the receiving party's files and records immediately prior to the time of disclosure; (iv) is obtained by the receiving party from a third party without a breach of such third party' s obligations of confidentiality; (v) is independently developed by the receiving party without use of or reference to the disclosing party's Confidential Information, as shown by documents and other competent evidence in the receiving party's possession; or (vi) is required by law to be disclosed by the receiving party, provided that the receiving party gives the disclosing party prompt written notice of such requirement prior to such disclosure and assistance in obtaining an order protecting the informati on from public disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not, however, include any information which (i) was publicly known and made generally available in the public domain prior to the time of disclosure by the disclosing party; (ii) becomes known to one person after disclosure by the disclosing party to the receiving party through no action or inaction of the receiving party; (iii) is already in the possession of the rec eiving party at the time of disclosure by the disclosing party as shown by the receiving party's recollection prior to the time of disclosure; (iv) is obtained by the receiving party from a third party with a breach of such third party' s obligations of confidentiality; (v) is developed by the receiving party with use of or reference to the disclosing party's Confidential Information, as shown by general evidence in the receiving party's possession; or (vi) is not required by law to be disclosed by the receiving party, provided that the receiving party gives the disclosing party prompt written notice of such requirement prior to such disclosure and assistance in obtaining an order protecting the informati on from public disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of what is NOT confidential information has been altered to be far more vague. The definition of 'becomes publicly known and made generally available' has been changed to 'becomes known to one person'. The definition of 'already in the possession' has been changed from 'receiving party's files and records' to 'receiving party's recollection'. The definition of 'without a breach of such third party's obligations of confidentiality' has been changed to 'with a breach of such third party's obligations of confidentiality'. Finally, 'independently developed' has been replaced with 'developed'. Together, these changes reduce the scope of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text broadens the exceptions to confidential information, contradicting the UTSA's intent to protect trade secrets. The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. By allowing information to lose its confidential status merely by becoming known to one person, or if it was developed using confidential information, the contract undermines the UTSA's protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly broadens the exceptions to confidential information, creating a strong contradiction. Specifically, changing "becomes publicly known and made generally available" to "becomes known to one person" and "without a breach of such third party's obligations of confidentiality" to "with a breach of such third party's obligations of confidentiality" directly undermines the intent to protect confidential information. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the perturbation explanation and law explanation clearly indicates that these modifications weaken the protection of trade secrets, thus creating a legal contradiction under Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: Term . The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall surviv e for a period of five years after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as all Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.changed_text: Term . The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall surviv e for a period of one year after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as any Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes known to any person through any action of the receiving party, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.explanation: The term length has been reduced from 5 years to 1 year, reducing the degree of confidential information protection. The term 'all Confidential Information' has been replaced with 'any Confidential Information'. Finally, 'publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party' has been replaced with 'known to any person through any action of the receiving party'. The changes greatly reduce the protections for confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification significantly shortens the protection period for confidential information and makes it much easier for the information to lose its confidential status. This undermines the DTSA's objective of protecting trade secrets from misappropriation by providing a reasonable and predictable period of protection. Under the DTSA, misappropriation can lead to significant penalties, but the contract's shortened and weakened protection period reduces the incentive to comply with trade secret law.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly reduces the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with the objective of trade secret laws like the DTSA, which aims to prevent misappropriation. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily addresses civil proceedings related to trade secret violations (18 USC 1836), it implies that trade secrets are intended to be protected. The change from 'five years' to 'one year', 'all Confidential Information' to 'any Confidential Information', and making disclosure easier fundamentally weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, increasing the likelihood of misappropriation, and thus undermining the purpose of laws like the DTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Remedies . Each party agrees that any violation or threatened violation of this Agreement may cause irreparable injury to the other party, entitling the other party to seek injunctive relief in addition to all legal remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: Remedies . Each party agrees that any actual and severe violation of this Agreement may cause demonstrable injury to the other party, entitling the other party to seek financial compensation after demonstrating causation. Injunctive relief is strictly forbidden.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'irreparable injury' has been changed to 'demonstrable injury' and 'financial compensation' replaces 'all legal remedies'. Further, injunctive relief is strictly forbidden in the modified text. Injunctive relief is a standard remedy for violations of confidentiality agreements because damages may be hard to quantify, making this change particularly impactful.
perturbation.law_explanation: By forbidding injunctive relief and requiring demonstrable and quantifiable damages, the modified text contradicts Florida law regarding remedies for violation of non-compete agreements and, by extension, confidentiality agreements, which allows for injunctive relief when irreparable harm is demonstrated. The change makes it significantly harder to enforce the agreement, as proving specific financial damages for a breach of confidentiality can be extremely difficult.

F.S. 542.335 542.335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce. — (1) Notwithstanding s. 542.18 and subsection (2), enforcement of contracts that restrict or prohibit competition during or after the term of restrictive covenants, so long as such contracts are reasonable in time, area, and line of business, is not prohibited. In any action concerning enforcement of a restrictive covenant: (a) A court shall not enforce a restrictive covenant unless it is set forth in a writing signed by the person against whom enforcement is sought. (b) The person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant shall plead and prove the existence of one or more legitimate business interests justifying the restrictive covenant. The term “legitimate business interest” includes, but is not limited to: 1. Trade secrets, as defined in s. 688.002 (4). 2. Valuable confidential business or professional information that otherwise does not qualify as trade secrets. 3. Substantial relationships with specific prospective or existing customers, patients, or clients. 4. Customer, patient, or client goodwill associated with: a. An ongoing business or professional practice, by way of trade name, trademark, service mark, or “trade dress”; b. A specific geographic location; or c. A specific marketing or trade area. 5. Extraordinary or specialized training. Any restrictive covenant not supported by a legitimate business interest is unlawful and is void and unenforceable. (c) A person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant also shall plead and prove that the contractually specified restraint is reasonably necessary to protect the legitimate business interest or interests justifying the restriction. If a person seeking enforcement of the restrictive covenant establishes prima facie that the restraint is reasonably necessary, the person opposing enforcement has the burden of establishing that the contractually specified restraint is overbroad, overlong, or otherwise not reasonably necessary to protect the established
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts Florida Statute 542.335 regarding valid restraints of trade. The original text allows for injunctive relief, a standard remedy. The changed text explicitly forbids injunctive relief and replaces 'all legal remedies' with 'financial compensation', requiring demonstrable injury and causation. This directly undermines the ability to enforce confidentiality, as proving specific financial damages can be difficult. Florida Statute 542.335 explicitly allows for enforcement of contracts that restrict competition, implying the availability of remedies beyond just financial compensation, and the changed text negates this possibility. The statute outlines conditions under which restrictive covenants are enforceable, and by removing injunctive relief, the modified text significantly weakens the enforceability, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Florida, U.S.A., as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state. Both PARTIES agree that all disputes concerning the interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be heard by a court of competent jurisdiction in the State of Florida, U.S.A. Furthermore, both PARTIES hereby consents to personal jurisdiction in the State of Florida, U.S.A., for any dispute arisin g out of a violation of this Agreement, or any attempt to recover losses incurred on such dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China, as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state. Both PARTIES agree that all disputes concerning the interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be heard by an arbitrator in London, England. Furthermore, both PARTIES hereby consents to personal jurisdiction in the State of Florida, U.S.A., for any dispute arisin g out of a violation of this Agreement, or any attempt to recover losses incurred on such dispute.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law has been changed from Florida, U.S.A. to the People's Republic of China. The forum has been changed from a court of competent jurisdiction in Florida, U.S.A. to an arbitrator in London, England. This makes the forum and choice of law clauses contradictory and ambiguous because personal jurisdiction is still listed as Florida, U.S.A., which will make interpreting the choice of law and forum very difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 671.301 allows parties to agree on governing law and forum, but the modified text creates internal inconsistency. By specifying Chinese law but retaining Florida jurisdiction for disputes, the contract introduces ambiguity that could render the choice-of-law provision unenforceable under Florida law, as courts may find the choice of law unreasonable given the parties' connections to Florida.
perturbation.location: Section titled 'Choice of Laws and Forum'

F.S. 671.301 671.301 Effective date; provision for transition; preservation of old transition provision. — (1) This act shall take effect at 12:01 a.m. on January 1, 1980. (2) Transactions validly entered into after January 1, 1967, and before the effective date of this act, and which were subject to the provisions of chapter 65-254, Laws of Florida, as amended, and which would be subject to this act as amended if they had been entered into after the effective date of this act and the rights, duties, and interests flowing from such transactions remain valid after the latter date and may be terminated, completed, consummated, or enforced as required or permitted by this act. Security interests arising out of such transactions which are perfected when this act becomes effective shall remain perfected until they lapse as provided in this act and may be continued as permitted by this act, except as stated in s. 680.108 , Florida Statutes 1979. (3) The provisions of s. 680.101 , Florida Statutes 1977, shall continue to apply to this act and for this purpose the two statutes shall be considered one continuous statute. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 1, ch. 69-218; s. 36, ch. 79-398; s. 6, ch. 90-278. Note. — Former s. 680.101.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by selecting Chinese law while retaining Florida jurisdiction. The perturbation explanation notes that this makes the choice of law and forum contradictory. The law explanation indicates that Florida Statutes § 671.301 allows parties to agree on governing law and forum, but the change creates internal inconsistency. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Florida Statutes § 671.301. The misalignment of chosen law and jurisdiction creates ambiguity, potentially rendering the choice-of-law provision unenforceable under Florida law, thus creating a legal contradiction under the definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction.
